And really, it's about starting to build buzz ahead of the launch. This is where you lay the groundwork for your launch. Now, we usually start off with what I call the shot across the bow. This is an old naval term, and it would be like, if one Navy ship is chasing down another ship, or maybe it's a Coast Guard ship chasing down a boat, they would often fire a shot in front of the other boat, land a shot in the water just to warn them that something's coming. So I like to call this the shot across the bow. Now, the essence of the pre-pre is that you're letting your market know that something is coming, but you're not actually trying to sell them. In fact, a big part of your message is that the product is not quite ready yet. So I mean, if the product's not even ready yet, you can't actually be selling them, right? So you remember that BS detector that we talked about when I taught you about the sideways sales letter. And the thing is, is the BS detector, there's that red zone, you want to avoid that red zone. And when you're in the pre-pre, and you're just, you'll see that it's very easy to avoid that red zone in the pre-pre, because you're not actually even selling anything to anyone. And when you start off with that BS detector in the safe zone, it's easier to keep it in that safe zone. You know, when there's no hint of a pitch, and you tell people that something's coming, and you're really excited about it, it's like you might come out and say, hey, we've been working on this thing for years, we're so excited, I can't wait to bring it to you, can you just help us out by filling out this survey before we release this? And that is a lot friendlier than something like, hey, we've been working on this thing for years, I'm really excited about it, and please buy it for me, give me your money. ## [09:02 - 12:04]

that which they help create. This is a key principle. People like to support that which they help create. So if they give you some feedback, if I go out to my followers here and say, hey, I'm almost done with this product, but I wanna know, I wanna make sure I cover everything in this product, can you tell me what your number one question is about growing a list, about learning how to play rhythm guitar, about how to build your massage practice, whatever you are in, if you go out and ask people for feedback, and what they need to learn to really help change their lives, then by giving you feedback, you've enlisted them in the cause. They are now part of the creation, and people like to support that which they help create. So if they feel like they're helping you create the product, they're gonna be more inclined to tell people about what you're doing, to talk about to you, to rush to your defense if anyone's saying bad things about you, and then eventually to get involved in your whole pre-launch process, and then maybe even buy your product. So getting that feedback early on is really, really important. A little mystery in your pre-pre, a little mystery goes a long way. A lot of times I'll try to structure my pre-pre-launch, so I'm dropping breadcrumbs here and there, and people try to piece it together, and once they piece it together, then it's almost like a scavenger hunt, and once they start to put the pieces together, they love to tell others about it. They love having the feeling they've got some insider knowledge, but having insider knowledge is only great if you can actually go and share it with someone. So people are gonna feel great about connecting the breadcrumbs together, and now they've got a secret, and then they wanna go out and share it. If you can string those breadcrumbs together and create a little mystery, it can go a long ways towards getting the buzz really moving in your launch and your pre-pre. I like to think about, in Malcolm Gladwell's book, the tipping point, he talked about mavens and connectors, and mavens are people that just love to collect data and information. They like to become expert. Connectors are people that they know lots and lots and lots of other people. They have big followings in social media. A lot of people that you do see publishing very actively in social media, they are connectors. They really enjoy being connectors. So those mavens and connectors are people that I'm trying to get hooked into my pre-pre and get them excited. I also think about persons of influence within my market or niche when I'm sort of in this pre-pre. Who can I, who is a real influence?
